text: Honestly, I go away on holiday for a few
days and what feels like the biggest
controversy in pop culture blows up
across the internet. Out of all the
movies I expected to be a lightning rod
for conspiracy theories, political
tugofwarss, and the kind of explosive
fan warfare that makes the Snider versse
look like a polite disagreement over
afternoon tea. The last one I predicted
was James Gun Superman by James Gunn.
But not to worry, dear viewer, because
the drinker is back to bring order out
of chaos. restore balance to a world
gone mad and pronounce the final verdict
on James Gun's Superman by James Gun.
And damn, man, what a delightfully
optimistic, frustratingly flawed, and
thoroughly weird movie this turned out
to be. On the plus side, the casting is
excellent. David Corinswe turned out to
be a perfect choice for The Man of
Steel. The script doesn't try to
diminish or deconstruct him just to
massage some smug filmmaker's nihilistic
ego, but instead presents him as a
flawed but good-hearted man doing his
best to navigate a complex world.
There's a strong theme of heroism and
positivity running through the movie, a
refreshing lack of political agenda, and
some genuinely great dramatic moments
that made me think, "Yep, James Gun's
Superman by James Gunn actually
understands what people want from a
Superman movie." In short, its heart is
definitely in the right place. And when
James Gun manages to get out of his own
way, he's able to deliver a strong take
on Superman that feels fresh and
different, but still vital and timeless.
That being said, it's still very much a
James Gun movie with all the baggage
that entails. The plot is a
schizophrenic mess that drops you right
into the middle of a story that's
halfway told already. The humor that
worked so well in the Guardians movies
now feels forced, dated, out of place,
and gratingly annoying. There's way too
many characters for the script to
sustain them all, and the story hinges
on a big lore change in Revelation that
I'm pretty sure is going to piss off
comic book fans. Basically, James Gun's
Superman by James Gun is a bit of a
mess. A creative and well-meaning mess,
but a mess all the same. neither great
nor terrible, but a flawed creation
based on flawed ideas and a perfect
example of why just throwing a bunch of
talented people together with no thought
for their creative sensibilities doesn't
always guarantee a great end products.
Anyway, I'll give you a brief idea of
the story so you know what you're
dealing with here. The film ditches the
usual origin story and instead picks up
a few years into Superman's career where
he's recently intervened to prevent a
war in Eastern Europe, for now at least,
and is busy fighting a losing battle to
Ultra Man. But it turns out this was all
just a distraction by Lex Luthther so he
could find the Fortress of Solitude and
steal information that would allow him
to defeat Superman. I mean, considering
what Ultra Man is eventually revealed to
be, he already has everything he needs
to take Superman down, but whatever.
Let's just go with it. Anyway, Luther
eventually finds and decodes a message
from Jorel telling Superman to conquer
and subjugate the peoples of Earth,
which naturally doesn't go down too well
when it gets released to the public.
Superman's forced to turn himself into
the US government so he can try to clear
his name. But instead, he gets handed
over to Luther, who sends him to a
pocket universe that he created to hold
superpowered prisoners because
apparently that's a thing you can just
do now. You sure about that?
You sure about that? Oh, yeah. And if
you were following the leaks, this is
also where he keeps his army of mutated
monkeys chained to computers who work
night and day to stoke anti-Supman
resentment on social media. Yes, this is
real. This is a thing which is in the
movie,
right? Honestly, a couple of dozen guys
in an office in India could have done
the same job with less effort. But what
can I say? It's James Gun Superman by
James Gun. Also, you'd think the ability
to create entire universes would kind of
negate the need to fight one man for
control of a tiny fraction of one
planet, but again, whatever. Just don't
think about it too much. Anyway, with
Superman out of action, it's up to Lois
Lane to join forces with the Justice

which is in this movie.
You are now a life member of the Groovy

to clear his name and find a way to

Now, you probably don't need me to tell
you that there was a [ __ ] ton of
expectation riding on this movie. Not
only did it have to vindicate James Gun
as the new head of the franchise, but it
also had to relaunch a whole new version
of Superman, kickstart the entire DCU,
and save the future of superhero movies
single-handeds. Because let's be honest
here, something tells me the Fantastic 4
isn't going to get the job done. It was
a pretty tall order to begin with, and
to be honest, I'm not sure it
accomplishes any of those things, never
mind all of them. Like I said before,
there's definite positives to be drawn
from this film, so don't go thinking
it's all doom and gloom. David Corinwat
was a solid casting choice for Superman.
He's earnest and sincere. He's
definitely got the right look for the
character, and he plays well off Rachel
Brosahhan as Lois Lane. In the hands of
a different creative, a modern-day Lois
could have come across as a smug,
abrasive girl boss, but she never does.
She challenges him on his beliefs at
times, but it feels like it comes from a
well-intentioned place and not some
insecure need to assert moral
superiority over him. Krypto the
superdog was another divisive aspect of
the trailers. Some people loved him,
others hated him, but really, he's not a
big deal in the movie at all. He shows
up from time to time when the script
needs him and then conveniently vanishes
when it doesn't. The Justice Gang are a
bit of an odd choice, though. Apart from
Mr. Terrific, they don't have much to do
except fight the old monster in the

off because Nathan Fillian deserves

like Mark Melin. But Agatha is the

I'm happy to say that James Gun's
Superman by James Gun absolutely isn't a

As Nathan Fillian says,
just a movie, guys.
The only problem, though, is that it's
not a particularly good Superman movie,
and a lot of that problem stems from
James Gunn himself. I think it's about
time people stop worshiping this guy
like he's some cinematic messiah sent
down to save Hollywoods. His recent
track record has been spotty at best.
And although he's definitely good at
what he does, what he does only works
within a pretty narrow comfort zone. And
if he's forced to step too far outside
it, he seems kind of lost and
uninspired. I get the feeling he would
have much rather made a Justice Gang
movie because it would have played to
all his favorite sensibilities as a
filmmaker, forcing another bunch of
likable misfits to work together to take
down a bigger threat. But there was too
much writing on this film for him to
trust it to anyone else. And so we ended
up with a Superman movie that can't
quite seem to keep its focus on
Superman. Another shortcoming of Guns
that's become more obvious in recent
years is the humor. When it comes to
writing dialogue, Gun is basically a
second generation follower of Weedenism.
Much like Taikoiti, he seems to
understand what made Jos Weeden's
writing so effective in superhero movies
and definitely knows how to replicate it
effectively. But what he lacks is
Weeden's finely tuned sense of
restraint. A bit of carefully crafted
levity at just the right moment can
really enhance a dramatic scene, but too
much at the wrong time soon becomes an
irritating distraction. And that's
exactly what Superman suffers from.
Every time the movie's on the brink of
some intense, heartfelt moment, it's
almost always undercut by some shitty,
badly executed joke, like it's afraid to
ever take itself seriously. Not every
movie has to be Guardians of the Galaxy,
James. You're allowed to move on in
life. There's just so much [ __ ] thrown
at you in this film, so many characters
that don't get the time and space they
need to breathe and develop, so much
worldchanging technology and truncated
subplots that it becomes kind of
overwhelming after a while. By the time
the credits rolled, I actually felt
tired of it all. It's a film that never
quite finds its narrative focal point
that's in such a rush to introduce so
much new stuff that it ends up getting
in its own way. And it doesn't help that
it feels like we're dropped into a story
halfway through the telling. Like there
should have been another movie before
this one to properly introduce Superman,
Lex Luthther, and the Justice Gang.
Instead, they're all just thrown into
the mix without any explanation. Like,
we're just expected to know who and what
everyone is. And yeah, I know most
people know who Superman and Lex
Luthther are by this point, but this
isn't just a new version of these
characters we're seeing here. It's a
complete relaunch of the world that
they're part of. It should be epic and
significant, but instead it just feels
like the middle entry of a trilogy or a
by the numbers mid-face Marvel movie,
just business as usual. Just another
forgettably mids superhero flick that
happens to have Superman in it. Add all
these problems together and you end up
with a film that's somehow overshadowed
by its own creator that can't seem to
get out of its own way and that fails to
live up to the impossible expectations
placed on it. Superhero movies really
have reached a fork in the road of
destiny this year and desperately needed
something to get them on the right path
again. The Marvel juggernaut is fallen
to pieces. Sony have abandoned whatever
the [ __ ] they were hoping to accomplish
with the leftovers, and DC have been
unsuccessfully relaunched and reinvented
more times than Katy Perry. At this
point, none of it seems to be working.
And as much as this movie makes a
valiant effort, I don't think James
Gun's Superman by James Gun is going to
turn it around.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
